title-group.article-title: Association of COVID vaccinations and treatments with long COVID beyond 6 months: a case-control study on the adult population in a large integrated healthcare system in the United States from 2020 to 2023

contrib-group.aff: aUniversity of Texas Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, TX, United States
bWashington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, MO, United States
cDepartment of Research and Evaluation, Kaiser Permanente Southern California Permanente Medical Group, Pasadena, CA, United States
dGraduate Medical Education Administration & Research, Kaiser Permanente Southern California Permanente Medical Group, Pasadena, CA, United States
eDepartment of Family Medicine, Kaiser Permanente Southern California Permanente Medical Group, Santa Ana, CA, United States

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author at: 1900 4th Street, Santa Ana, CA 92705, United States. diane.y.jerng@kp.org

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: Long coronavirus disease (Long COVID) is a chronic condition causing significant long-term disability and economic burden. This study aims to measure the association between COVID vaccinations and treatment at the time of acute infection with Long COVID outcomes beyond six months post-infection, controlling for demographic and medical variables.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This retrospective case-control study used electronic medical records from Kaiser Permanente Southern California to evaluate Long COVID outcomes from January 2022 to June 2023 with vaccination doses and nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, and from October 2020 to June 2023 with remdesivir and other treatments. Statistical analysis used univariate chi-square and Kruskal-Wallis tests and conditional logistic regression.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Our study had 840 (January 2022 analysis) and 2632 (October 2020 analysis) Long COVID patients diagnosed by International Classification of Diseases-10 code at least 6 months after acute COVID infection with 1:5 matched controls by age, sex, race/ethnicity, Body Mass Index, and date of acute COVID infection. Long COVID outcome was inversely associated with the number of COVID vaccination doses (one dose odds ratio (OR) 0.77; 95 % CI 0.63–0.96, two doses OR 0.73; 95 % CI 0.59–0.92, three doses OR 0.64; 95 % CI 0.44–1.00, four doses OR 0.29; 95 % CI 0.06–1.49) and nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (OR 0.05; 95 % CI 0.04–0.07) or remdesivir (OR 0.31; 95 % CI 0.19–0.49) treatment with no interaction between three vaccinations with nirmatrelvir/ritonavir and four vaccinations with remdesivir treatment.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: These findings may influence existing clinical practices for vaccination and antiviral treatment strategies to decrease Long COVID consequences.

list-item.p: Addresses vaccination and treatment impact on Long COVID outcome beyond six months.
Increasing number of vaccinations further decreases Long COVID 23–36 %.
Remdesivir effect was independent of up to four vaccination doses.
Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir effect was independent of up to three vaccination doses.

def.p: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Long coronavirus disease (Long COVID) is “an infection-associated chronic condition that occurs after severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection and is present for at least 3 months as a continuous, relapsing and remitting, or progressive disease state that affects one or more organ systems.” (Examining the Working Definition for Long COVID | National Academies, 2024) An estimated 6 % of the global population infected with coronavirus disease (COVID) suffer with Long COVID (Global Burden of Disease Long COVID Collaborators, Sarah Wulf Hanson, Cristiana Abbafati, Joachim G. Aerts, Ziyad Al-Aly, Charlie Ashbaugh, Tala Ballouz, 2022). Studies have found increased risk of Long COVID with repeated COVID infections, potentially increasing Long COVID incidence over time (Bowe et al., 2022). Health care costs and reduction or loss of employment costs range up to $3.7 trillion in the United States alone (Cutler, 2022). Thus, there is a desperate need to understand preventive measures for Long COVID.

sec.title: Rationale
sec.p: COVID vaccinations and treatments are modifiable factors, which makes them useful preventive measures. COVID vaccination has been associated with a reduced risk of Long COVID; however, vaccination benefit spans a wide range from 4 to 84 % risk reduction (Al-Aly et al., 2022; Arjun et al., 2022; Byambasuren et al., 2023; Kuodi et al., 2022). In addition, although anti-viral results varied widely (Ioannou et al., 2023; Durstenfeld et al., 2024; Xie et al., n.d.; Wang et al., 2024; Fernández-de-las-Peñas et al., 2024; Boglione et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2025), a meta-analysis of all anti-viral treatments also concluded that Long COVID was on average 27.5 % lower in the antiviral treatment group, including remdesivir, nirmaltrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid), and molnupiravir (Choi et al., 2023). However, most of the existing studies evaluated Long COVID at or less than 6 months, a time period that some patients are still naturally recovering from their symptoms to complete resolution (Montoy, 2023). This self-recovery process can skew the existing research results on vaccine and treatment efficacy for the group of Long COVID patients who continue to suffer more than 6 months after their acute COVID infection. Because Long COVID has been demonstrated to extend months and years beyond the initial 3-to-6-month period, it is important to differentiate the impact of vaccination and treatments on Long COVID risk for this group of Long COVID people who suffer this prolonged disability (Montoy, 2023).

Moreover, no publications to our knowledge to date directly address the interaction of vaccinations and antiviral medications. To address these issues and further clarify the utility of vaccinations and treatments for risk reduction of Long COVID, we employ a case-control design within a large integrated healthcare electronic database, accounting for known confounding factors cited in the literature, and using physician/clinician diagnosis by International Classification of Diseases 10 (ICD-10) codes of Long COVID 6 months and beyond after acute COVID infection (Duerlund et al., 2022).

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To test whether the number of COVID vaccinations received (zero to five doses) prior to acute COVID infection and the receipt of COVID treatment at the time of acute COVID infection is associated with Long COVID outcomes 6 months and beyond post-infection.

sec.title: Study design and setting and study population
sec.p: This case-control study was conducted within the Kaiser Permanente Southern California (KPSC) integrated healthcare system in the United States and approved by the Institutional Review Board of Kaiser Permanente Southern California. Patient records link patient data over time and through enrollment and non-enrollment periods. We included patients with data at the time of acute COVID infection and Long COVID diagnosis coded 6 months until the end of the study period.

Our electronic medical record database tracks COVID infections by SARS-CoV-2 positive lab results, ICD-10 codes for COVID infection, patient reported positive home test kits, patient request for acute COVID treatment and work notes, chart review search words for COVID/SARS-CoV-2 infection, and reported care received outside of our healthcare system.

Cases were patients diagnosed with Long COVID whose symptoms persisted for greater than 6 months after an acute COVID infection. In cases with multiple COVID infection, the index COVID infection was the most recent COVID infection that was at least 6 months prior to the first ICD coding for Long COVID. Controls without Long COVID have a corresponding positive COVID test result on the exact date of case infection.

A chart review was performed to validate the ICD codes used to distinguish between cases and controls. 125 case patients and 75 control patients were randomly selected for a physician manual chart review to verify physician/clinician accuracy of Long COVID diagnosis (Appendix A). Some patients were assigned a Long COVID ICD code that subsequent visits did not substantiate. Therefore, patients were defined as Long COVID cases only if they had two or more ICD-10 coded Long COVID diagnosis 6 months and beyond an acute COVID infection and/or Long COVID diagnosis coded as an ongoing chronic medical condition in the Problem List. This adjustment helped to avoid inaccurate diagnosis or self-resolving symptoms that may have been mis-attributed to Long COVID. The Long COVID patients who continued to have ongoing Long COVID symptoms 6 months and beyond from their acute COVID infection were selected as cases with this ICD criteria adjustment. Patients who only had an ICD code for Long COVID within 6 months of their index COVID infection were excluded from the Long COVID case cohort. All 75 controls in the chart review did not have any Long COVID diagnostic codes or associated symptoms by chart review word search.

KPSC patients from 18 years old and older with a COVID positive result or documented COVID infection from January 1, 2022 to November 30, 2022 were included to evaluate the effect of vaccinations and nirmatrelvir/ritonavir treatment on Long COVID outcomes. This study period spans the time after primary COVID vaccination series with booster was available and use of nirmatrelvir/ritonavir began in our organization. The Long COVID diagnostic codes for Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) (U09.9/B94.8, G93.31/R53.82, U09.9/B94.8, U09.9/B94.8, U09.9/B94.8, U09.9/B94.8, U09.9/B94.8, U09.9/B94.8) were abstracted from June 1, 2022 to June 30, 2023 to capture Long COVID diagnostic codes 6 months to 1 year from the acute COVID infection.

Since nirmatrelvir/ritonavir treatment during the January 1, 2022 to November 30, 2022 study period was being used in place of remdesivir and other COVID treatments when criteria were met (Appendix B), a second analysis was performed when these other treatments were available. KPSC patients from 18 years old and older with a COVID positive result or documented COVID infection from October 1, 2020 to November 30, 2022 were included to evaluate the effect of remdesivir and other available treatments on Long COVID outcomes. Long COVID diagnostic codes were abstracted from April 1, 2021 to June 30, 2023 to capture Long COVID diagnostic codes 6 months to 2.16 years from the acute COVID infection.

A sensitivity analysis was conducted with KPSC patients from 18 years old and older with a COVID positive result or documented COVID infection from March 1, 2020 to November 30, 2022 when COVID testing became widely available.

Patients with a history of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) (ICD-10 code R53.82) or fibromyalgia (ICD-10 code M79.7) fulfilling ME/CFS criteria were excluded. We did not include pediatric patients in this study as the number of Long COVID patients less than 18 years old was small and anti-viral treatment usage was rare.

sec.title: Case-control matching
sec.p: This study used matched cohorts (1:5 case: control) to age, sex, ethnicity, and body mass index (BMI), as well as the exact date of acute COVID infection to account for different SARS-CoV-2 variant periods (Appendix B). For categorical age groups, we used 18 to less than 45, 45 to 55, and greater than 55 years old. Sex was female or male. Ethnicity was grouped into Asian, Black, LatinX, White, and Other. BMI was grouped categorically as normal (less than 25), overweight (25 to 30), and obese (greater than 30). These demographic factors have been implicated to have impact on risks for Long COVID (Vimercati et al., 2021; Tsampasian et al., 2023).

sec.title: Variables
sec.p: The primary exposure variable was number of vaccination doses at the time of index COVID infection. Completion of three vaccination doses was available at the initiation of our January 2022 study period, with the primary series beginning December 2020 and the third booster dose available October 2021. The fourth vaccination dose was available April 2022, within six months after the third dose. The fifth vaccination dose was available September 2022, within six months after the fourth dose. We did not include vaccination categories defined as Full, Partial, or None (Appendix B), because our sample size for fully vaccinated Long COVID patients was small. Only patients with Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccinations were used in our study because of its wide availability in our healthcare system.

A secondary exposure variable was the dispensed use of inpatient and outpatient treatment of acute COVID infection with remdesivir, monoclonal antibodies, convalescent plasma, or nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, which were the most used treatments at the time (Appendix B). Our pharmacy database tracks dates of inpatient or home infusion initiation and dispensed outpatient medications within our pharmacy and outside KPSC-affiliated pharmacies. Monoclonal antibody and convalescent plasma treatment was variable in availability and efficacy through this study period, and use was discontinued December 1, 2022 in our healthcare system.

Other covariates included hospitalization at the time of the index COVID infection, number of COVID infections, comorbid asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), diabetes, hypertension (HTN), depression, anxiety, and smoking.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Continuous variables were compared using Kruskal-Wallis test. Categorical variables were analyzed using chi-square test. A conditional logistic regression analysis examined the association between exposures to COVID vaccinations and treatments at the time of the index COVID infection and Long COVID outcome. Based on significant variables and adequate sample size, the following variables were included along with number of COVID vaccination and acute COVID treatment with nirmatrelvir/ritonavir and remdesivir: hospitalization, number of COVID infections, and comorbidities (asthma, COPD, smoking status, diabetes, hypertension, depression). Odds ratios (OR) are reported with 95 % confidence intervals.

An interaction study to analyze the maximum likelihood estimates was also conducted to evaluate any interaction between vaccination doses and treatment effect for nirmatrelvir/ritonavir from January 2022 to June 2023 and remdesivir from October 2020 to June 2023.

Age groups 18–49, 50–64, and 65+ were used in the conditional logistic regression model to correspond to updated Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) guidelines for vaccination and anti-viral treatment.
All statistical analysis was performed using statistical software SAS 9.4.

sec.title: Study size
sec.p:
The total population in our database included 1,118,736 controls and 6546 cases that met our definition of Long COVID with symptoms beyond 6 months (Appendix C). 15,553 patients were excluded from our unmatched total population due to ME/CFS or fibromyalgia diagnosis. After matching demographic factors (age, sex, ethnicity, BMI, and date of acute COVID infection), we studied 840 cases and 4200 controls for the January 2022 to June 2023 analysis (Table 1a), and 2632 cases and 13,160 controls for the October 2020 to June 2023 analysis (Table 1b).Table 1a
Demographic information, treatment, and comorbidities split between Long COVID (coronavirus) cases and COVID positive controls for matched adult population in Kaiser Permanente Southern California from January 2022 to November 2022.Table 1a

Chi-Square test and matched by age, sex, race/ethnicity, Body Mass Index (BMI), acute coronavirus (COVID) infection variant date.

sec.title: Descriptive statistics
sec.p: Participants with Long COVID were less likely to be treated with nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (9.9 % vs 44.8 %; p < 0 0.001) (Table 1a) and remdesivir (0.8 % vs 2.4 %; p < 0 0.001). Convalescent plasma (0.5 % vs 0.3 %, p = 0.37) and monoclonal antibody (0 % vs 0.1 %, p = 0.07) treatment were not significantly different between groups (Table 1b).

sec.title: Conditional logistical regression analysis
sec.p:
The number of COVID vaccinations was inversely associated with Long COVID outcome compared to those who had no COVID vaccinations; odds ratios for Long COVID were reduced among those with one dose (OR 0.77; 95 % CI 0.63–0.96), two doses (OR 0.73; 95 % CI 0.59–0.92), and three doses (OR 0.66; 95 % CI 0.44–1.00). Patients with four doses showed a trend only for reduced odds of Long COVID compared to those who had no vaccination (OR 0.29; 95 % CI 0.06–1.49) (Table 2a). There were no patients with five vaccination doses in our Long COVID cases. Treatment with nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (OR 0.05; 95 % CI 0.04–0.07) at the time of the index COVID infection was associated with decreased Long COVID outcome (Table 2a). Treatment with remdesivir (OR 0.31; 95 % CI 0.19–0.49) at the time of the index COVID infection was associated with decreased Long COVID outcome (Table 2b).Table 2a
Adjusted odds ratios from conditional logistic regression with 95 % confidence intervals for vaccination and nirmatrelvir/ritonavir effect on Long COVID (coronavirus) outcome from matched adult population in Kaiser Permanente Southern California with acute COVID infection January 2022 to November 2022.Table 2a

Our sensitivity analysis for KPSC patients from 18 years old and older with a COVID positive result or documented COVID infection from March 1, 2020 to November 30, 2022 when COVID testing became available revealed comparable results (Table 3).Table 3

40.310.06–1.56
Remdesivir Given⁎0.310.20–0.50
Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir Given⁎0.060.05–0.07
Medical History
Hospitalization⁎1.521.12–2.07
Number of COVID-19 Infections1.891.75–2.03
Prior Asthma⁎1.211.03–1.43
Prior COPD⁎1.291.07–1.55
Prior Depression⁎1.411.21–1.66
Prior Hypertension⁎0.840.73–0.96
Prior Diabetes⁎0.890.77–1.04
Smoking StatusCurrent1.00
Former1.451.18–1.78
Never1.140.98–1.34⁎
Compared to patients without the variable.

There was no interaction found between nirmatrelvir/ritonavir and three vaccination doses (p = 0.38 for one vaccination, p = 0.24 for two vaccinations, and p = 0.08 for three vaccinations). For four vaccination doses, there was an interaction found, p = 0.002. There was no interaction found between remdesivir for four vaccination doses (p = 0.74 for one vaccination, p = 0.92 for two vaccinations, p = 0.96 for three vaccinations, p = 0.98 for four vaccinations).

When using the 18–49 age group as a reference in the January 2022 to June 2023 analysis, the 65 and older age group had a reduced odds of Long COVID (OR 0.48; 95 % CI 0.31–0.75) and the 50–64 age group had a nonsignificant result (OR 0.76; 95 % CI 0.54–1.07). When using the 18–49 age group as a reference in the October 2020 to June 2023 analysis, the 65 and older age group had a reduced odds of Long COVID (OR 0.59; 95 % CI 0.46–0.78 and the 50–64 age group had a nonsignificant result (OR 0.90; 95 % CI 0.75–1.09).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our case-control study using an electronic database adjusted for demographic and other confounding variables was consistent with other studies finding a reduced risk of Long COVID after vaccination, nirmatrelvir-ritonavir, and remdesivir treatment.

Since a universally accepted definition of Long COVID is still controversial and being established, different definitions of Long COVID used across studies may affect data outcomes (Appendix A). Our study differed from most studies in that we assessed Long COVID conditions after 6 months, a distinction that has been supported by more recent studies (Montoy, 2023; Thaweethai et al., 2023). Significant natural reduction and resolution of symptoms can occur up to 6 months at which point Long COVID symptoms seem to stabilize (Montoy, 2023). Interestingly, Long COVID with post-viral chronic fatigue and ME/CFS share similar features and the Institute of Medicine of the National Academies recommends 6 months of symptoms before the diagnosis of ME/CFS is made (Komaroff and Lipkin, 2023; Wong and Weitzer, 2021; IOM, 2015). Using at least 6 months of Long COVID symptoms in our study clarifies the impact of intervention and limits confounding scenarios that include individuals whose symptoms continue to decrease and resolve naturally 4 weeks to 6 months from their acute COVID infection.

sec.title: Vaccinations
sec.p: Our findings support other studies in that one vaccination dose decreases Long COVID, but an increasing number of vaccine doses further reduce Long COVID outcomes (Byambasuren et al., 2023). Patients with four vaccination doses showed no statistical significance, likely due to inadequate power in this Long COVID cohort. The small number of Long COVID cases in our data of four or more vaccination doses as well as in our fully vaccinated cohort may also reflect a vaccination protective factor which could not be addressed in our case-control design. In our total population of over one million patients prior to matched data, there were only 3 patients in our Long COVID cohort with 4 vaccinations and no Long COVID patients in our cohort who had 5 vaccinations, the maximum number of vaccinations recommended by CDC at the time of our study period (Appendix C).

sec.title: Treatments
sec.p: For the impact of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir on Long COVID outcomes, there was a significant decrease in Long COVID at a greater impact than other studies examining Long COVID at or less than 6 months (Wang et al., 2024; Fernández-de-las-Peñas et al., 2024; Boglione et al., 2022; Choi et al., 2023; Xie et al., 2023). In our January 2022 to June 2023 analysis, remdesivir effect on Long COVID outcomes was not statistically significant, likely from the small number of patients treated with remdesivir during this time period. When we analyzed the impact of remdesivir on Long COVID outcome in our October 2020 to June 2023 analysis, the results were similar to a study on Long COVID risk at a mean of 18 months post-acute COVID infection with a risk reduction of 60 % on Long COVID (OR 0.40; 95 % CI 0.26–0.63) (Fernández-de-las-Peñas et al., 2024).

Moreover, this study provides new evidence that there was no significant interaction between the effect of vaccination up to three doses and nirmatrelvir/ritonavir treatment and up to four vaccination doses with remdesivir treatment. This result supports strong protection against persistent Long COVID symptoms with anti-viral treatment for all adults who continue to decline COVID vaccination due to adverse side effects, allergic reactions, or vaccine hesitancy. Moreover, these findings not only support the importance of vaccinating widely but also providing antivirals for vaccinated individuals to significantly reduce Long COVID outcomes. These results are consistent with findings by Xie, Choi, and Al-Aly (2023b) who examined the effect of nirmatrelvir/ritonavir treatment and also found a 26 % reduction of Long COVID in patients with at least one risk factor for progression to severe COVID-19 illness at 6 months follow-up in “unvaccinated, vaccinated, and boosted, and in people with primary SARS-CoV-2 infection and reinfection” (Xie et al., 2023).

In contrast to antivirals showing a greater reduction in Long COVID outcomes, our study was inconclusive for plasma transfusion and monoclonal antibody treatment on Long COVID. Since plasma transfusion and monoclonal antibody treatment were not consistently available or widely used in our organization, our sample size was small. In contrast, a prospective study in a randomized treatment trial demonstrated early COVID convalescent plasma (CCP) treatment less than 5 days after symptom onset compared to late CCP treatment had statistically significant lower odds of Post COVID Conditions at 90 days post-infection (Gebo et al., 2023).

sec.title: Limitations and next steps
sec.p: This research study used a case-control design, which has inherent limitations because causality cannot be established. In addition, our sample may not be representative of all patients who were undiagnosed, misdiagnosed, died, or left our organization through the COVID pandemic. In addition, patients may have filled nirmatrelvir/ritonavir prescriptions but may not have subsequently taken the medication.

Because we designated the index COVID infection as the most recent COVID infection documented at least 6 months prior to the first ICD coding for Long COVID, it is possible that a second infection occurring within the 6 months of the Long COVID diagnosis was the infection that caused the Long COVID diagnosis. The incorrect COVID infection may have been attributed to the Long COVID diagnosis in our data for these instances.

This design also could not address incidence or prevalence, so we could not evaluate the decreased risk of Long COVID that vaccinations may affect by preventing or mitigating COVID infection. Vaccination and treatment recommendations as well as different times of availability varied throughout the course of the COVID pandemic, which may have limited sample size and power for direct comparison. It may be important in future studies to investigate whether four or more doses of vaccination or vaccination status with booster dose within 6 months of acute COVID infection provides significant risk reduction of Long COVID. It would also be important to further study the pediatric population on vaccination and treatment impact for Long COVID beyond 6 months.

There were a few patients (2 %) in our chart review study who had Long COVID symptoms for more than 6 months but recovered after a year, having received several vaccinations within the first 6-month period after acute infection. We did not analyze the impact of COVID vaccination after acute COVID infection or other factors on resolution of Long COVID symptoms beyond 6 months since this was out of the scope of our study. However, our observation is supported by studies showing that an initial early vaccination after acute COVID infection may decrease the risk of ongoing Long COVID symptoms (Al-Aly et al., 2022; Kuodi et al., 2022; Herman et al., 2022; Malden et al., 2024). However, results from other studies on vaccination after Long-COVID diagnosis to improve Long COVID symptoms revealed conflicting outcomes of improvement, same, or worsening status (Scherlinger et al., 2021; Wanga, 2021; Tran et al., 2021; Arnold et al., 2021; Strain et al., 2022; Massey et al., 2021; Ceban et al., 2023). Further studies exploring the effects of vaccination after acute COVID infection could clarify outcomes of Long COVID and provide another strategy in controlling the incidence rate of Long COVID.

Our findings may support more widespread use of antiviral treatment to reduce the risk of Long COVID than current clinical practice. Future study to explore the patient characteristics in each of the vaccination and treatment exposure and non-exposure groups would further enlighten our understanding of Long COVID risk factors to guide future clinical practice guidelines.

sec.title: Summary
sec.p: Our case-control study of adults in a large electronic healthcare database, accounting for existing known confounding factors and using physician/clinician diagnosed Long COVID by ICD-10 code, advances previous studies that vaccination, remdesivir, and nirmatrelvir/ritonavir show significant associations with decreased Long COVID outcomes at 6 months and beyond after acute COVID infection. Moreover, our interaction study findings suggest that broader use of anti-viral treatment may greatly decrease the risk of future Long COVID disability with its associated personal, societal, and economic suffering.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Curtis Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis. Celina Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis. Rui Yan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Davida Becker: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jia Xiao Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jeffrey Slezak: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Diane Jerng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: The Institutional Review Board of Kaiser Permanente Southern California reviewed and approved the protocol on 4/13/2022 #13206. Patient consent was not deemed necessary.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This research was supported by the Regional Research Committee of 10.13039/100005977Kaiser Permanente Southern California [grant # KP-RRC-20230502] from July 1, 2023 to June 30, 2024.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

element-citation.part-title: Nirmatrelvir and the Risk of Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19. Published online November 5, 2022

sec.label: A.1
sec.title: Criteria for long COVID with chronic fatigue symptoms
sec.p: Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 with Chronic Fatigue (U09.9).

Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 with Post-viral Fatigue Syndrome (G93.31)•
Symptoms persisting past 6 months after acute COVID infection, AND•
Decreased level of functioning (occupational, educational, social, or personal activities) compared to prior to COVID infection, AND•
Symptoms of post-exertional malaise/fatigue (PEM): not completely relieved with sleep, exacerbated by physical or mental exertion, requiring a recovery time, AND•
At least one of the following persistent or recurring neuroimmune symptoms:o
cognitive (i.e., brain fog, attention, concentration, memory, receptive and expressive communication)o
exertional pain (i.e., headache, myalgias, arthralgias, chest pain, abdominal pain)o
sleep disturbances (i.e., insomnia, falling/staying asleep, naps)o
vestibular (i.e., balance, tinnitus, vertigo, hearing)o
mood changes (i.e., depression, anxiety)o
sensory processing (i.e., photophobia, phonophobia, odor/texture sensitivity, taste changes)o
dysautonomia (i.e., breathing, palpitations, dizziness, temperature/sweat dysregulation, thirst, swallowing, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, pupillary, urinary, sexual, neuropathy, tremors, abnormal movements)o
immune activation (i.e., rash, swelling, sore throat, lymphadenopathy, environmental sensitivities), AND•
At least one of the following persistent or recurring COVID infection system symptoms:o
pulmonary, cardiac, neurologic, dermatologic, endocrine, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, ocular, olfactory/gustatory, renal, vascular/hematologic, immunologic, or psychiatric symptoms AND•
No other condition causing symptoms.

sec.label: A.2
sec.title: Criteria for long covid with organ/system symptoms
sec.p: Post Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 with Respiratory Symptoms (U09.9).
Post Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 with Heart Symptoms (U09.9).
Post Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 with Neurological Symptoms (U09.9).

Post Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 with other symptoms (U09.9)•
Symptoms persisting past 6 months after acute COVID infection, AND•
Decreased level of functioning (occupational, educational, social, or personal activities) compared to prior to COVID infection, AND•
At least one of the following persistent or recurrent COVID infection system symptoms:o
pulmonary symptoms (PASC with Respiratory)o
cardiac symptoms (PASC with Heart)o
neurologic symptoms (PASC with Neurological)o
dermatologic, endocrine, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, ocular, olfactory/gustatory, renal, vascular/hematologic, immunologic, or psychiatric symptoms (PASC with Other symptoms), AND•
No evidence of end-organ/system pathology/inflammation from COVID infection•
No other condition causing symptoms.

sec.label: A.3
sec.title: Criteria for long COVID with end-organ/system sequelae.
sec.p:
Late Sequelae of COVID infection (U09.9)•
Symptoms persisting past 6 months after acute COVID infection, AND•
Decreased level of functioning (occupational, educational, social, or personal activities) compared to prior to COVID infection, AND•
At least one of the following persistent or recurrent COVID infection system symptoms:o
pulmonary, cardiac, neurologic, dermatologic, endocrine, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, ocular, olfactory/gustatory, renal, vascular/hematologic, immunologic, or psychiatric symptoms, AND•
Evidence of end-organ/system pathology/inflammation from COVID infection:o
pulmonary, cardiac, neurologic, dermatologic, endocrine, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, ocular, olfactory/gustatory, renal, vascular/hematologic, immunologic, or psychiatric symptoms, AND•
No other condition causing symptoms.

sec.label: A.4
sec.title: Criteria for post COVID recovery
sec.p:
Post COVID-19 Condition (U09.9)•
Symptoms persisting past 4 weeks after acute COVID infection, AND•
Complete resolution of COVID infection system symptoms, AND•
Complete recovery to normal level of functioning (occupational, educational, social, or personal activities), AND•
No evidence of end-organ/system pathology/inflammation from COVID infection, AND•
No other condition causing symptoms.
*Patients may have Long COVID prior to 6 months but meet the clinical criteria at 6 months since there is higher rate of full recovery from ongoing symptoms prior to 6 months.
**Patients may have multiple or concurrent Long COVID diagnosis at different periods of time.
***Criteria may be used in conjunction with the U.S. National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine definition of Long COVID.

sec.label: B.2
sec.title: Antiviral inclusion criteria
sec.p: Remdesivir inclusion criteria included COVID symptoms within 7 days, with priority given to patients with presence of risk factors, including unvaccinated individuals, older age, and risk factors for severe disease progression. Risk factors may include asthma, cancer, cerebrovascular disease, children with underlying conditions, chronic kidney disease, chronic lung disease, chronic liver disease, cystic fibrosis, diabetes, Down's syndrome, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, cardiomyopathy, HIV, hypertension, immune deficiencies, immunosuppressive medications including steroids, mood disorders including depression, neurologic conditions including dementia, schizophrenia, current or former smoking, BMI greater or equal to 25, pregnancy and recent pregnancy, sickle cell disease, solid organ or blood stem cell transplantation, substance use disorders, thalassemia, tuberculosis.
*Remdesivir was not used for age less than 28 days old and weight less than or equal to 3 kg.
*Although current guidelines cite 7 days within symptom onset, remdesivir was often used past 7 days for hospitalized patients during the inception of the pandemic.
Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) inclusion criteria included symptoms within 5 days, with priority given to patients with presence of risk factors (stated above in remdesivir inclusion criteria).

*Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir was not used for age less than 12 years old, weight less than 88 pounds, persons with certain solid organ transplants or bone marrow transplants or other immunosuppressed state, and circumstances where remdesivir treatment is recommended.

sec.label: B.3
sec.title: Vaccination status
sec.p: We define full vaccination as 2 weeks after complete primary vaccination series doses (Moderna or Pfizer, 3 doses) AND within 4 months of vaccination OR completed the most recently available booster vaccination. Partial vaccination was defined as any number of vaccinations not fulfilling full vaccination criteria or no vaccination criteria. No vaccination included patients with no COVID vaccinations.

sec.label: Appendix C
sec.title: Demographic statistics, treatment, and comorbidities comparing Long COVID (coronavirus) cases and COVID positive controls for unmatched total adult population in Kaiser Permanente Southern California from March 2020 to June 2023

tr.td: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease prior to COVID

fn.p: Kruskal-Wallis p-value; all other p-values used Chi-Square.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: Stephanie Tovar, MS, Manager, Program Management, Clinician Research Division, Department of Research & Evaluation, Kaiser Permanente Southern California Permanente Medical Group.
